subject: Human Resources
title: Anchoring Bias in Salary Negotiations
description: Learn Anchoring Bias in Salary Negotiations in one minute.

hook.text: A candidate says: 'I'm currently making $120K.' The recruiter anchors the offer at $125K—even though the role's budget was $150K.

buildup.text: Anchoring bias causes people to rely too heavily on the first piece of information they encounter when making decisions.

discovery.text: In salary talks, whoever sets the first number sets the anchor—all subsequent discussion revolves around that starting point.

twist.text: Salary history bans exist in many states because anchoring perpetuates past underpayment—especially for women and minorities.

climax.text: HR counters anchoring by setting pay ranges based on role value first—then negotiating within the range, not from history.

punchline.text: The first number spoken in a negotiation has outsized power. Choose it carefully.

quiz.question: Why have many states banned salary history questions?
options: Because anchoring to past pay perpetuates underpayment of women and minorities
To prevent employees from earning too much
